data.title: FMI International Equity-Hedged Composite Strategy Q3 2025 Review
data.description:
Investment Philosophy

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

Investment Process

We utilize a business owner's approach to investing, thor- oughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the character- istics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns-on-invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are re- sponsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typical- ly includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropri- ate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is dis- cussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 2-4%. The portfolio generally consists of 25-40 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum. Since inception, the strategy has generally hedged a significant portion of its currency.

Recent Purchase:

Fluidra S.A. Ticker: FDR SM Added: September 2025

Fluidra is a leading pool equipment manufacturer and distributor. The company estimates that about half of its business comes from repair/replacement sales, around a third comes from outfitting newly built pools, and the remainder comes from pool remodels. Higher interest rates, soft housing markets, and weakening economies around the world have pressured discretionary pool spending. The trade war has also caused sentiment to sour because many of the products sold in the U.S. are manufactured in both Mexico and China. We believe Fluidra should be able to manage through tariffs by increasing price, negotiating with suppliers, making changes to its supply chain, and through other productivity measures. While we're not attempting to call a bottom in new pool construction, we believe the long-term outlook for this industry remains strong, and their shares appear to be attractively valued. We initiated a 1.7% position in the portfolio in September 2025.

Recent Sale:

Safran S.A. Ticker: SAF SP Sold: August 2025

Safran is a manufacturer of aerospace engines and equipment. Safran's engine business is primarily a joint venture with General Electric (GE) called CFM International. CFM has a commanding position in the narrow body jet engine market, with most large narrow body aircraft over the last 30 years being powered by the CFM56 engine. The company began producing the newer LEAP engine in 2016, which now has market shares of ~60% and 100% on the Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) 320 and Boeing (BA) 737MAX, respectively. Safran's profits are primarily driven by the aftermarket. We initiated a position in the company in 2018 and held the company through the COVID downturn. As aerospace markets have recovered, the shares have followed suit. We had been trimming our position into the strength and sold our remaining 1.0% position in August 2025.

data.title: Advance Auto Parts: Turnaround Is Progressing (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Advance Auto Parts (AAP) are up 25% over the past year, though performance has been very volatile as investors gauge what has been a choppy turnaround effort. Indeed, shares are down over 20% from their recent high as concerns have mounted about discretionary consumer spending and potential cost pressures from tariffs that may be difficult to pass on. These concerns led me to rate shares a sell in August, and since then, the stock has lost 14%, and shares are now trading around my $48-50 fair value. Given this and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Advance Auto Parts.

Margin improvement has been impressive

In the company’s third quarter, reported on October 30th, Advance Auto Parts earned $0.92, which beat estimates by $0.17 even as revenue fell by 5% to $2 billion. This decline in revenue is really due to the impact of store closures earlier this year. Same-store sales were actually up 3% from last year. Adjusted results exclude $57 million of one-time costs in the quarter, primarily related to ongoing efforts to restructure and improve the business. AAP generated a 4.4% operating margin in Q3, up 370 bps from last year.

Frankly, there were encouraging signs in this quarter as operational improvements took hold. Adjusted gross margins expanded 260 bps to 44.8%, despite the pressure of tariffs. It seems AAP has been able to pass on much of this cost to consumers. On the earnings call, management said that AAP raised prices about 3% from last year, and the number of transactions was down slightly. This was up slightly from the 2% inflation in Q2 as tariff costs continue to work their way through the supply chain. Management expects a further sequential increase in prices in Q4 to 4%, with a further increase in Q1 of next year, at which point it should begin leveling out.

These price increases are primarily to offset costs rather than intended to expand margins. Competition remains elevated, reducing the capacity to raise prices too much, especially in a mixed consumer environment. Management sees positive sales in the Pro channel while do-it-yourself activity is choppier. Pro same-store sales were up 4%. In contrast, AAP is seeing pressure from low and mid-income households as they more carefully watch budgets amid persistent inflation pressures.

With the cost of routine jobs increasing, management sees a risk that some consumers are taking longer to do regular maintenance work (i.e. extending an oil change to 8,000 miles from 7,500). Ultimately, I see the DIY channel continuing to underperform the pro channel. That said, a recession is not my base case, and a likely larger tax refund season in Q1 (given changes to tax law this July) could help demand recover. I do not see consumer spending surging by any means, but these factors mitigate the risk of decline.

In-store improvements take hold

Importantly, AAP’s self-help measures are gaining traction. Product availability in stores has improved about 500 bps and is running in the 96-97% area. AAP is now approaching its high 90% availability target. Investments in supply chain management and SKU optimization are paying off, and having in-stock availability is essential to maintain customer relationships as few things are more frustrating than going to a store only for the desired product to be out of stock. Along these lines, AAP has also invested in improving the in-store experience by enhancing staffing. The time-to-serve customers has improved by over 10 minutes but is still a bit above the 30-40 minute target.

Even with these changes, SG&A of $823 million was 40.4% of sales. This was down 110bps from last year given stronger sales at stores that remained open. SG&A spending was down nearly 8% from last year. This expense performance has been very encouraging, given the fact that Advance Auto Parts has also been able to improve the customer experience at the same time.

Cash generation is muted

AAP also has its balance sheet in a reasonable position. Net leverage ended the quarter at 2.6x. The company ended the quarter with $3.2 billion of cash, $3.4 billion of debt and $1.9 billion of operating leases. Cash is elevated given ongoing restructuring costs. There have been $130 million of cash costs associated with its restructuring efforts this year, and I see ~$500-$800 million of cash needs over the next two years. This includes its 2% dividend yield. Its dividend costs about $60 million per year.

That said, inventories are up $72 million this year to $3.7 billion, even with sales running lower. This reflects the need to improve inventory availability after struggling with low in-stock levels in prior years. Free cash flow is -$277 million so far this year as working capital has been a drag, which is consistent with the typical seasonal pattern. In fact, working capital has been a $460 million headwind this year.

As you can see below, it raised the low end of its sales expectations while moving its operating margin and EPS outlooks closer to the prior midpoints. Even as it cut its cap-ex spending, free cash flow is likely to be a bit worse than previously expected, pointing to somewhat weaker working capital recovery in Q4.

Shares are fairly valued

Back in August, I was targeting $4.50-$5.00 in 2027 earnings. Management is targeting a 7% operating margin over the medium term, which could support over $6 in EPS, but I remain cautious about this occurring. Much of the supply chain savings has already occurred, and I am cautious about the ability to move prices much higher without sacrificing transaction volumes. Still, the company clearly has momentum, and margins should get past 5%.

AAP is delivering on its own operational goals, significantly improving the in-store experience, which should help it gain market share. That said, the underlying macro environment is mixed at best, and this is likely to limit potential sales growth. Plus, this company is unlikely to generate enough cash flow to cover its dividend until 2027 at the soonest. In other words, the turnaround is clearly gaining traction, but it will be a long road to complete the turnaround.

I still view ~$4.50-$5.00 as the company’s steady-state earnings power, which should be achievable in two years. At 10x 2027 earnings, with capital returns beyond the current dividend unlikely for some time, I struggle to see scope for much further multiple expansion. That said, shares are now more appropriately factoring in the length of the turnaround. As such, I am upgrading the stock to a “hold.” I would rather see shares at the lower-$40s before actively buying, but there is no pressing need to sell after recent underperformance.

data.title: After The Renaissance: Crypto Meets Its Reckoning
data.description:
When we wrote nearly a year ago about crypto’s “renaissance” in the wake of President Donald Trump’s return to the White House, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) had just surged past $95,000 and the digital asset complex was basking in glory with a total market cap exceeding over $3 trillion.

Deregulation was back in vogue, the SEC had finally approved spot Bitcoin ETFs, and the Trump campaign’s embrace of crypto gave believers something they had always craved: political capital at the highest echelons of the U.S. government.

Yet the reality of the past twelve months has proven more complicated. The same political environment that once set the stage for Bitcoin’s rise has now become a sobering lens through which we evaluate the structural weaknesses that never fully disappeared. While crypto enjoyed its moment in the sun, the subsequent correction has forced a reconsideration of what the past year has truly achieved.

Euphoria Peaks and the Market Turns

Bitcoin’s dramatic surge above $125,000 early in the autumn was fueled by growing acceptance across Wall Street, record inflows into spot Bitcoin ETFs, and a continued belief that the administration was preparing to treat Bitcoin as a strategic national asset. The optimism felt powerful and at times rather eerily reminiscent of earlier boom cycles observed in 2017 and 2021.

The CoinDesk 20 Index, which measures the performance of the 20 largest and most liquid digital assets, has dropped over 21%

But within weeks the momentum shifted. As interest rate expectations shifted and tariff concerns with China lingered, Bitcoin prices declined. Additionally, as macroeconomic concerns resurfaced with a subdued labor market and questions about the state of the consumer economy, prices fell further, dropping to nearly 87,000 by mid-November.

More than a trillion dollars in notional value vanished across the market. Liquidations swept across leveraged positions, and the same cryptocurrency traders who had celebrated the rally as a sign of lasting structural change found themselves confronting once again the unforgiving nature of crypto’s sentiment-driven cycles.

The Limits of Political Sponsorship

These developments revived questions that lingered beneath the surface even during the most exuberant moments of the Trump era. Chief among them is whether political friendliness can truly substitute for the kind of regulatory clarity the industry has long avoided.

The administration’s embrace of crypto elevated the narrative and helped inspire a flood of new capital, yet it could not insulate the market from broader economic forces. Political warmth cannot change the mechanics of leverage, liquidity, or macro cycles.

The proposal for a national Bitcoin reserve remains emblematic of this tension. It captured imaginations and later generated executive directives, but its practical framework remains unresolved.

Agencies continue to study its implications, Congress has taken no decisive action, and the long-term consequences for the dollar and the global financial system remain unclear. The idea may continue to serve as a symbol of support, yet symbols alone cannot sustain a market that remains highly sensitive to economic stress.

Lessons of FTX and the Persistence of Old Patterns

The structural risks that came into view after the FTX collapse have not entirely disappeared. Courts delivered billions in restitution and judgments over the past year, marking an important step toward accountability. But the system that produced those vulnerabilities remains largely intact.

Leverage has not meaningfully declined. Perpetual futures still dominate exchange trading volumes. Open interest remains concentrated on a small group of exchanges that are themselves exposed to sharp swings in liquidity.

The rapid unwinding of positions during the latest correction demonstrated how easily a single shock can cascade across the ecosystem. While regulation has intensified in some areas, the underlying market structure continues to amplify volatility. The industry has moved away from the most obvious failings of the FTX era but has not yet built the guardrails needed to prevent similar dynamics.

The ETF Era and its Complications

The rise of Bitcoin exposure through ETFs has reshaped the investment landscape. These products have attracted institutions and have shifted trading from offshore venues to regulated platforms. This shift represents real progress compared with the environment that existed only a few years ago.

Still, several risks remain. Many of the most popular Bitcoin ETFs are structured as trusts or commodity pools rather than as registered investment companies. They provide familiarity and ease of access, yet they do not offer the full set of investor protections associated with 1940 Act funds.

These distinctions matter when markets become stressed. ETF flows can deepen liquidity but can also magnify volatility when sentiment reverses abruptly.

Taken together, these developments reveal a market that has matured in some respects while remaining vulnerable in others. The political environment is friendlier, institutional infrastructure has improved, and the industry now occupies a more secure place within the public financial imagination. Yet the behavior of prices over the past year indicates that crypto still relies heavily on momentum and the pursuit of extraordinary returns.

The very forces that drove Bitcoin’s ascent this year also enabled its sudden decline. As long as sentiment remains the primary engine of market direction, investors should expect cycles of exuberance and retrenchment to continue.

What Comes Next

It is tempting to view the past year as the moment when digital assets finally broke from their old cycles, yet the evidence points in a different direction. Rather than behaving like an emerging store of value or a counterweight to economic stress, crypto has increasingly moved in step with the rhythm of high beta equities.

Its rallies have been strongest when liquidity was abundant and risk sentiment was elevated, and its downturns have been most severe when macro conditions tightened. The pattern resembles the behavior of a speculative growth stock rather than an asset with defensive qualities, underscoring how far the market still is from achieving the stability its advocates often claim.

This alignment with broader risk markets helps explain why the industry’s structural vulnerabilities remain so visible. A more reliable pricing framework, one grounded in verifiable reserves, and transparent market structure, would go a long way toward reducing these pressures.

If matched with legitimate and recurring use cases that extend beyond trading and speculation, the resulting foundation could strengthen crypto’s claim to durability and soften the extreme swings that continue to define the asset class.

One year ago, we noted that the industry stood at a crossroads. That remains the case today, perhaps even more clearly, which is one of the structural reasons The Milwaukee Company, of which TMC Research is an affiliate, has refrained from allocating to digital assets.

While the Trump era has created openings for digital assets, it has also revealed that political support cannot counteract the force of economic cycles. The question now is whether the industry can mature into something more resilient or whether it will continue to behave like a high beta vehicle that rises quickly in good times and falls just as fast when the environment shifts.

The answer will depend on whether crypto can finally build the transparency, governance, and real world utility that would allow it to evolve from a speculative instrument into a lasting component of the financial system.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: BBJP: Better Than EWJ, But Not Advisable To Pursue Now
data.description:
Introduction

Investors who are desirous of ETF vehicles that cover equities from the land of the rising sun, are currently faced with close to 20 different alternatives. Within this landscape, our focus today will be on the JPMorgan BetaBuilders Japan ETF (BBJP), which comes across as one of the more prominent Japanese ETFs around, with total assets under management [AUM] of over $14B.

What Does the BBJP ETF Do?

BBJP currently covers around 190 Japanese stocks from the Tokyo and Nagoya stock exchanges, and an overwhelming majority of the former’s portfolio consists of giant and large caps (almost 93% of the portfolio).

Note that BBJP doesn’t actively pick these 190-odd stocks but rather passively follows an index called the Morningstar Japan Target Market Exposure Index [MJTMEI], which is a market-cap-weighted index (so naturally the largest stocks take up the most weight).

BBJP does a pretty decent job of tracking MJTIEI, as its tracking errors across different time periods show that it is a tad lower than what other ETFs experience. Low tracking errors are a must for passively managed products; otherwise, it defeats the whole purpose, and they might as well just actively pick stocks. Nonetheless, BBJP does well on this front because, unlike a lot of other passively managed ETFs, which primarily resort to representative sampling, it follows a full replication policy in its efforts to follow MJTEI.

How Does BBJP Compare To Its Largest Rival- EWJ?

To get a better sense of BBJP’s strengths and weaknesses, we felt it would be appropriate to measure it against the iShares MSCI Japan ETF (EWJ), which is widely perceived to be the foremost ETF in the Japanese landscape. Part of this is because EWJ tracks the famed MSCI Japan Index [MJI], which is generally relied on by global portfolio managers as the prime benchmarking gauge of Japanese equities.

EWJ is certainly more of a stalwart than BBJP, having been around for almost three decades, whereas the latter has been in existence for only around 7 years. But don’t dismiss BBJP’s growing popularity; despite coming to the bourses two decades after EWJ, its AUM total has steadily progressed and is now only around $1B lower than EWJ, which has seen its AUM fall by over 20% since BBJP got listed.

What could explain BBJP’s growing popularity?

Well, for starters, there are the fees. BBJP tracks a Morningstar-based index whose licensing fees are generally lower than index licensing fees associated with MSCI indices, which are relied upon by the institutional community. This puts BBJP in a better position to pass on costs to its retail clients, and you see that in a relatively low expense ratio of just 0.19%, which is not even half as much as what it costs to own EWJ (0.5%).

Then, while BBJP doesn’t see the same degree of daily dollar volumes as EWJ (a differential of almost 5x in favor of the latter), one can’t have too many complaints, when the spreads (the difference between the average bid and ask in percentage terms) of these two products are inordinately low at just 0.01%.

Even from an income angle, BBJP currently offers a slightly superior yield of around 30 bps, although some investors may prefer EWJ’s more frequent distributions (twice a year).

What’s relatively common between these two products is that they cover a roughly same number of stocks (around 190), which is largely tilted towards giant and large-caps, and also the same sector (industrials, which take up one-fourth of the portfolio). Besides these two products, don’t churn their portfolio a great deal annually (low annual churn within the mid-single-digit terrain).

Another reason why BBJP has been gaining more fans at the cost of EWJ is also because it is doing a better job of delivering the goods in terms of returns.

On a total return basis, it is outperforming its larger peer by almost 300 bps, and crucially, on a risk-adjusted return basis, which is reflected by the Sharpe ratio (this metric tells us how much of excess returns a product is generating for every unit of standard deviation it faces), BBJP again comes out on top.

All in all, we feel BBJP is a superior product to its larger peer.

Is BBJP Worth Pursuing Now?

While we feel BBJP has an edge over EWJ for those looking to gain giant- and large-cap Japanese equity access, we don’t believe it would be the most opportune time to pursue this product.

In recent years, a lot has been made of Japan’s corporate governance reforms and the potential re-rating of the equity base, but we feel that theme has largely already played out. Note that BBJP’s holdings are now priced at a price-to-earnings multiple of 17x, which translates to a 15% premium over developed markets.

For such a relatively steep multiple (by developed market standards), one would like to see a better cadence of long-term earnings, but that isn’t what you get with BBJP (just 7% earnings growth potential, which also marginally lags developed markets).

We have also noted previously how Japanese businesses just have structurally lower profit margins that are not easy to overcome and which end up reflecting poorly on their ROEs [Return on equity]. The ROE is also a function of the asset turnover ratio (how effectively these businesses are utilizing their assets to generate business), and we not sure if this is going to pick up meaningfully, given the relatively weak position of Japanese industrials (which also dominate BBJP’s portfolio, accounting for a fourth of the total portfolio).

For instance, take a look at the latest forward-looking manufacturing PMI readings, a measure of factory order momentum, which saw its steepest decline in 20 months! Adding fuel to the fire are the Japanese PM’s latest comments against China, its largest trading partner, which have now prompted the latter to already ban seafood imports. For now, it is just seafood, but who’s to say it couldn’t spiral into something worse?

These developments come at a rather inopportune time, as Japanese exports had just only started growing after 4 straight months of decline!

Then, also consider that investors who move funds around within the developed markets won’t have any great incentive to rotate into Japan now, as its relative strength ratio has already mean reverted and is roughly on par with its long-term average.

Finally, another reason not to get too euphoric now is that the long-term price patterns of BBJP suggest that this product looks quite overextended now.

Basically, what we can see is that BBJP over time has moved up within the boundaries of a rising wedge (the two black lines). Within this wedge, the risk-reward does not look very ideal, as it is closer to the upper boundary. Secondly, also note that until the current month, BBJP had been closing outside its upper Bollinger Band (a measure of overextension, given it is 2 standard deviations higher than the 20-month average) for 3 straight months, but now there are signs of this reversing, particularly as the RSI indicator in the chart below also flagged overbought conditions last month.

All in all, to conclude, we’ll say we view BBJP as a reliable product to play the Japanese theme, but we don’t feel it is the best time to pursue it now.

data.title: Xunlei Limited: Dirt Cheap But No Near-Term Catalysts - Hold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Xunlei Limited, or "Xunlei" (XNET) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier article on the company.

Last week, Chinese digital media content Internet platform Xunlei Limited reported mixed third-quarter results with better-than-expected sales offset by declining margins:

The company recorded strong year-over-year and quarter-over-quarter growth across all business segments. Sales were boosted by the rapid expansion of Xunlei's overseas audio live-streaming operations and the recent $70 million acquisition of Hupu, China's leading sports media and data platform.

However, gross margins declined for a third consecutive quarter due to strong growth in the company's lower-margin audio live-streaming business and persistent margin pressure in the cloud computing segment.

In addition, increased marketing spend and the recent Hupu acquisition resulted in higher operating expenses.

Xunlei recorded a small profit from operations and generated approximately $8.0 million in cash. The company ended the quarter with $284.1 million in cash and liquid investments as well as $68.5 million in debt.

As expected, GAAP results were boosted by further price appreciation of the company's 7.8% stake in Chinese panoramic camera market leader Arashi Vision Inc. ("Arashi Vision"), better known as "Insta360".

As of September 30, Xunlei's stake in Arashi Vision was valued at approximately $1.3 billion, up from $0.75 billion at the end of June.

While Arashi Vision's shares have declined slightly in recent weeks, the investment is still worth close to $1.2 billion.

As a result, the company's enterprise value remains in deeply negative territory:

Unfortunately, the issue is not unique to Xunlei. Debt free Chinese online media and gaming platform Sohu.com (SOHU) commands $1.2 billion in cash and liquid investments, but its market capitalization is below $500 million.

That said, in contrast to Xunlei, the company has experienced substantial cash outflows in recent years, so this is not an apples-to-apples comparison.

Please note also that Xunlei's stake in Arashi Vision remains subject to a 12-month lockup agreement. As a result, the company will be precluded from selling shares until July 2026.

However, based on statements made by management on the conference call, I would still expect Xunlei to sell its stake in Arashi Vision next year (emphasis added by author):

We believe that future realization of investment gains will drastically strengthen our balance sheet and provide strategic flexibility to pursue opportunities in research and development of new technologies, pursuing industry collaboration, selective investments as well as more options for the shareholder return.

But with Arashi Vision trading at approximately 9x estimated 2026 revenues and 60x estimated EBITDA, the company will have to execute flawlessly in order to preserve or even increase its lofty valuation.

While Xunlei's shares rallied to new multi-year highs above $11 in early October, the past six weeks have been tough:

While I would attribute some of the selloff to general market conditions and the decline in Arashi Vision's shares, last week's results and conference call have caused sentiment to deteriorate further.

During the questions-and-answers session of the call, management stated that there are no current plans for a sale of the Arashi Vision stake or the initiation of a dividend, which likely resulted in some investor disappointment.

But in my opinion, management simply tried to explain that Xunlei will remain precluded from selling its Arashi Vision shares for another couple of quarters.

In sum, things have not developed as hoped by me when I initiated coverage of the company with a "Strong Buy" rating three months ago.

While the investment thesis hasn't really changed, investor sentiment clearly has, thus making a near-term recovery rather unlikely.

Please note also that Xunlei's Q4 results are likely to show a meaningful GAAP loss, particularly if Arashi Vision's shares continue to drop.

However, I still expect the company to sell some or even all of its stake in Arashi Vision in H2/2026 at a substantial gain, which would increase the odds of a sizeable one-time dividend.

While Xunlei remains deeply undervalued, the lack of near-term catalysts (other than a potential rally in Arashi Vision's shares) is likely to weigh on the stock price.

Consequently, I am downgrading Xunlei Limited's stock from "Strong Buy" to "Hold" with a new price target of $9.00 (down from $13.70) based on even more conservative assumptions regarding the ultimate value of the company's stake in Arashi Vision next year.

Please note also that I have not assigned any value to Xunlei's core business.

In combination with the 70% discount applied to the company's stake in Arashi Vision, my valuation approach might be considered overly harsh, but with the overall market looking increasingly shaky, caution seems appropriate.

Given poor sentiment and lack of near-term catalysts, I have significantly reduced my position in Xunlei's shares over the past week.

Bottom Line

Xunlei Limited reported mixed third-quarter results with better-than-expected sales offset by persistent margin pressure.

After reaching a new multi-year high above $11 in early October, the stock price has declined by more than 40% in recent weeks due to deteriorating sentiment, a decline in Arashi Vision's share price, and the mediocre earnings report last week.

With the market on increasingly shaky ground and given the lack of near-term catalysts, I am downgrading Xunlei Limited's stock from "Strong Buy" to "Hold" while lowering my price target from $13.70 to $9.00.

data.title: NIO: Improving Fundamentals Suggest A Turnaround Is Taking Shape
data.description:
NIO (NYSE:NIO) is a Chinese EV developer and manufacturer. The company also provides innovative battery swapping technology and has more than 3,500 battery swap stations and roughly 4,800 charging stations in China.

The stock price is up 39% over the past 12 months and has significantly outperformed the benchmark. The EV company continues steadily growing its revenue, is achieving meaningful progress on the car delivery growth, and is well-positioned to lead the market over the following years.

Although the EV industry remains fierce with such names as Tesla, BYD, Li Auto, and others, I think the company's focus on immersive user experience and a well-connected network of battery swap stations argues for a bullish narrative and strong market position. I rate NIO a Buy with a $7 price target over the next 12 months, suggesting a 14% upside possibility and broader market outperformance.

FQ2 2025: Double-Miss, But Business Fundamentals Are Improving

On September 2, the EV manufacturer delivered FQ2 2025 results. Although this was a double-miss quarter, the company achieved progress in both the top and bottom lines. The market reacted to the results with a 3% rise in the stock price on the earnings day, but gave it all back over the following trading session, representing a 4% decline.

NIO posted $2.66 billion in revenue, suggesting a 9% growth on a year-over-year basis, but roughly $103 million below what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at negative $0.32 in diluted EPS, a progress versus negative $0.35 in the same quarter last year, but $0.03 below analysts' expectations.

In addition to this, on November 1, the company provided an October 2025 delivery update. The EV company has delivered 40,397 vehicles, signaling approximately 93% surge in performance on a year-over-year basis. On a year-to-date basis, the company delivered more than 241,000 EVs, representing about a 42% increase on a year-over-year basis. This indicates a solid momentum in demand, which argues for a continuation of the bullish narrative over the following quarters.

In the press release, the EV manufacturer indicated that vehicle sales reached $16.1 billion in the quarter, a roughly 3% increase on a year-over-year basis, and a 62% surge on a quarter-over-quarter basis. On the downside, NIO highlighted 10.3% in vehicle margin for the quarter, which suggests about a 20% decrease versus 12.2% in FQ2 2024. I think this remains an area to monitor for long-term investors, implying that if margins were to erode further, NIO is likely to continue trading at a suppressed valuation.

On the upside, the gross profit surged by 12.4% on a year-over-year basis, from $1.69 billion in FQ2 2024 to $1.89 billion this quarter. In addition, the gross profit margin achieved 10%, a 30 bps increase over the past 12 months. I think this indicates that the decline in vehicle margins remains a temporary hiccup due to the ramp-up of new vehicles, rather than a structural issue. On the overall business performance, fundamentals are improving, and that is a solid argument for a positive outlook.

NIO is experiencing strong demand for its new models, such as the ONVO L90, launched at the end of July this year. This highlights a strengthening position in the landscape. Moreover, at the time of the press release, the company's all-new ES8 was available for pre-orders. NIO delivered more than 6,700 vehicle units in October, and that is another positive tailwind, supporting top-line growth in the following quarters, if significant demand continues.

I would also like to emphasize that the global EV market was valued at $1.3 trillion in 2024, and is anticipated to roughly fivefold by 2030, reaching approximately $6.5 trillion. This suggests a 32.5% CAGR and suggests that if NIO continues growing at such a rapid pace, the EV manufacturer is likely to benefit from broader market growth.

Management guided for FQ3 2025. The company anticipates 89,000 vehicles at the midpoint, indicating approximately 44% growth on a year-over-year basis. The EV manufacturer is also aiming for roughly $3.1 billion in revenue at the midpoint, reflecting a high double-digit top-line growth of nearly 19% on a year-over-year basis. Given solid momentum in demand, I think the company can achieve these targets.

To conclude, although the company experienced a double miss versus analysts' estimates, the EV company's fundamentals are improving. The company reports top-line growth, an increase in gross profit, and margins. NIO's car deliveries are increasing, and this supports a positive outlook. I think management does a great job on execution, and NIO is exposed to multi-year tailwinds, which should drive the future revenue growth.

Although The Stock Rose By 39%, I Argue It Remains Undervalued And There Is More Room For Multiple Expansion

Given the fact that NIO is not a profitable company, it is difficult to evaluate whether it trades at an appealing level in terms of forward P/E. Based on the analysts' estimates, the EV manufacturer is anticipated to turn profitable in FY2028, and currently appears to be trading at 31x earnings multiple versus the FY2028 estimate.

I think NIO is a growth story and is likely to primarily focus on the top-line expansion over the following years; thus, the forward Price/Sales ratio appears to be a more reasonable metric. The EV company is currently trading at a Price/Sales ratio of 1.21, and that signals a 34% premium versus the sector median of 0.90. Although this could indicate limited upside in the future, I argue the stock is cheap if revenue growth delivers.

NIO is anticipated to reach $17.91 billion in revenue in FY2026, representing a 42% growth versus the FY2025 estimate. I think the company can achieve the target, supported by strong demand. If this materializes, the stock would trade at 0.85 forward P/S, which would indicate undervaluation versus peers. In addition to this, at such a rapid double-digit revenue growth pace, the market may be willing to pay a higher premium for robust performance and future tailwinds. This supports my bullish outlook, driven by outstanding performance. Moreover, the forward EV/Sales ratio of 1.44 implies roughly a 11% premium versus peers, and that appears insignificant if growth delivers.

On the downside, the company remains a medium-leveraged business. NIO has $4.32 billion in total obligations. Although the EV manufacturer has $2.52 billion in cash that partially offsets the debt, elevated leverage remains, and that led to nearly $127 million in interest expenses to service the debt.

The NIO is experiencing solid top-line growth, and that is fueling a bullish narrative. Over the past 12 months, the company achieved roughly a 9% revenue expansion, which reflects about 2.8x outperformance versus the sector median of 3.26%. The forward revenue growth rate of 32% argues that the trend is likely to continue, and that top-line expansion may actually accelerate. This also reflects a 10x outperformance versus peers at 3%.

The company operates at below-market margins, and that may cloud the future outlook. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 10% is roughly 3.8x below the sector median of consumer discretionary at 38%. To highlight, NIO is an EV manufacturer; thus, automotive companies are typically operating at suppressed margins. In addition to this, the net income margin (TTM) of negative 35% is significantly below the peers 4%. This also indicates that NIO's business model is capital-intensive, and high-volume scaling and new vehicle ramp-up are weighing on the profitability metrics.

To conclude, NIO is a top-line focused company, which is heavily expanding its vehicle delivery, resulting in significant top-line growth. The company is experiencing a solid momentum in demand, and I think it may remain a key tailwind in the future. China is supporting the EV industry through different subsidies, including direct manufacturing support and consumer incentives, which argues for a positive regulatory outlook, validating a bullish stance. NIO is also experiencing an improvement in margins, which may help bottom-line growth in the future.

NIO is trading at 1.21 forward P/S versus FY2025 Wall Street estimates, and at 0.85x versus FY2026 revenue expectations, representing a 42% top-line growth. To highlight, 0.85x indicates approximately 6% discount versus the sector median of 0.90x. Given the fact that the company is experiencing a robust business performance, I think it is well-positioned to achieve these revenue growth targets.

Furthermore, if the growth delivers, the market may be willing to price the stock at a higher premium. Therefore, I would not be surprised if NIO traded at 1.3x forward P/S further, due to excellent execution and solid momentum in demand. This would imply a nearly 50% growth versus the current forward P/S of 0.85x, accounting for the FY2026 revenue estimate. Although it may appear conservative, I think such an increase in forward P/S could translate to approximately 10% to 20% stock price appreciation. If we take 15% at the midpoint, we arrive at my price target of $7. This indicates continuation of the bullish momentum, improving fundamentals and business performance, yet a more conservative approach, which leaves room for error.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to NIO that could meaningfully impact the stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, although the EV global market is tied to substantial tailwinds, implying a fivefold expansion over the following years, the EV industry is fierce. NIO competes against Tesla, BYD, Li Auto, and other EV companies, and it may be a challenge to maintain a solid market share. If investors suspect that NIO loses its market position to competition, this could meaningfully impact stock price performance.

Although the company successfully improves EV delivery numbers, resulting in top-line growth, the EV manufacturer remains unprofitable. If the global economy enters a recession, consumers may cut their budgets and delay such purchases as a new vehicle. If this materializes, the company is likely to post weaker-than-anticipated results, which could cloud the future outlook.

The EV manufacturing industry remains capital-intensive and tied to execution risk. Although the company experiences positive reaction from consumers toward its new vehicle models, a sharp shift in sentiment, or more appealing models introduced by competition, could negative impact on NIO's performance. In addition, the company operates in a low-margin industry, and has constantly innovated to meet the market's demand, and that not an easy task to achieve.

On the other hand, if none of the risks and concerns materialize, NIO remains well-positioned to grow and benefit from the rise in demand.

Conclusion: NIO Is A Buy

I think NIO may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking diversification in a high-growth EV name that is strengthening its market position. NIO delivered a roughly double-digit top-line growth over the past year, and highlighted a solid demand in new vehicle launches, projecting a positive outlook.

The company remains unprofitable, and that could be concerning. The EV industry is capital-intensive, and although NIO remains medium-leveraged, this clouds the bullish narrative. On the other hand, the EV industry is supported by the local government, and the global market is anticipated to fivefold over the next years. If NIO can maintain its market position and grow revenue at such a rapid pace, it positions itself to benefit from broader market expansion.

That is why I rate NIO a Buy with a $7 price target over the next 12 months. This represents nearly a 14% upside possibility. 27 Wall Street analysts indicate a $6.91 12-month price target, suggesting a 12% upside potential. This is roughly in line with my projections, and it will be interesting to monitor whether tailwinds play out over the next year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Central Puerto: A Key Player In Argentina's Growing Energy Demand
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Mauro Caversazzi as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment thesis

Central Puerto S.A. (CEPU) is among the key Argentine companies that will supply the energetic demand of the future: It increased its generation from 14,419 GWh in 2021 to 21,605 Gwh in 2024. It could surpass that mark in 2025, producing 20,057 GWh in the first 9 months of the year.

To this operational growth, we must add the investment that OpenAI (OPENAI) announced to open a data center in Argentine Patagonia for an estimated amount of $25 billion. Both companies signed an MoU.

But this combination of great variables that reduced the undervaluation margins, doesn't change the investment attractiveness in the company, thinking of a long-term strategy. Besides, I still see the undervaluation multiples in the EV/EBITDA ratio of 7.2x against 12.8x of the sector, or the 8.7x median over the past 5 years.

Because the company's potential is huge, both for the Argentine energy (that is the main sector that generates revenues for the country, along with the agriculture sector) and for the domestic demand, I think that CEPU is too cheap regarding its future potential and that's why I rate it as a buying opportunity.

Operational Growth

For those that don't know the enterprise, its operativity and potential, I'd like to briefly highlight its growth projects. Central Puerto is one of the most important companies to Argentina's energy supply, covering 17% of the country's energy consumption with an annual generation volume over 20.000 GWh per year (at the moment they presented their Q3 results, generating exactly 20.057 GWh in this year). In the next chart I'll show you that the company has scaled its generation potential since 2021.

The base of this generation, with an installed capacity of 6.938 MW in 16 plants, that are mostly concentrated on thermal energy, represents 71% of its generation potential, while the hydroelectric power and the renewable energy represents 21% and 18%, respectively.

Power generation potential is still growing in the medium term thanks to the awarding of two battery storage projects, with a capacity of 205 MW based on lithium technology. These projects will represent an approximated CapEx of $140 million and they could be fully operational by 2027. To this interesting project we must add the recent capital investment that completed the operation of the solar park in Cafayate (Salta province) which added another 80 MW of installed capacity. (Son slide 2 of the Q3 Report).

Another part of capital investment in Q3 belongs to the maintenance of 2 plants (Brigadier Lopez and San Carlos) that are also increasing its installed capacity, and that will also concrete the final phase for its start in December, which, as the directive commission said, will impact in a very positive way both in Q4 and in 2026. The CEO, Fernando Bonell, said in the earnings call, that these two projects project an increase of the EBITDA of $70 million. With all of these investments (even without the OpenAi investment, which we'll discuss later), the projection is that Adjusted EBITDA will grow 35% for 2028 (on slide 4 of the Investor Presentation). This approximately yields in an Adjusted EBITDA of $430 million, because in the last 9 months of the year, the company has generated an Adjusted EBITDA of $317 million.

OpenAI's Investment and the Role of Central Puerto

The announcement of OpenAI's investment of nearly $25 billion to open a data center in Patagonia is a historic bet for the Argentine economy. Let's add some perspective. Argentine exports in 2024 represented over $79 billion, which means that the announced investment of $25 billion is equal to nearly 33% of Argentine exports in one year. While there's obviously a difference between a one-time investment and output, the numbers are still very large. All in all, this equals something close to 4% of Argentina's GDP, which is a bit over $630 billion, just to give you an idea of its scale.

The thing with the installation of a data center in Patagonia is that this idea is clearly great, because they will easily adapt to the cold and dry weather (ideal for the refrigeration system), if Argentina has the energetic infrastructure that is required to supply the demand. This isn't a very different problem to the debate over the present and future data centers in the US that will demand a very high installed capacity of MW.

We still don't have enough official information about the progress of this investment, fundamentally the information about where it will be built. With that information we could better estimate the potential beneficiaries. Neuquen province, where the unconventional Vaca Muerta shale formation is, is a possibility. In that province, Central Puerto operates the hydroelectric plant Piedra del Aguila, the biggest third one of the company because of its installed capacity for 2026 of 1.440 MW.

The firm that leads the project in Argentina, based on what the company said on October 10th, is Sur Energy. It mediates between Argentine companies and Sam Altman's startup, while Argentina's government also promotes its development. The other company that would join Central Puerto is Genneia S.A., which specializes in the generation of renewable energy.

By talking about these things I want to highlight that Central Puerto could be a very important piece in the development of this data center. AI is a very important tool for all its end users, but the indirect beneficiaries that aid in its production could also be winners.

But let's take the worst case scenario. Let's suppose that CEPU doesn't participate in supplying of the energy required by OpenAI, does it mean it will be detrimental for the company? My answer is no because the entire energy sector in Argentina has growing demand, so there will be new investment opportunities even if this project falls through with Central Puerto. Remember the growth projection for 2028 of Adjusted EBITDA, so even without that investment, shows that the company will remain very important for future energy demand.

3 key takeaways from Q3

Growth and Margins

More opportunities in the local energy market mean operating growth, with healthy debt levels. This encourages me to rate this company as an attractive buying opportunity, because I perceive that it is in a phase of solid, ongoing operational growth since Q1: we just have to look at the installed capacity since 2018.

Let's go now to Q3. Net income was $104 million against $39 million of the same period the previous year (+8% YoY) and revenue was $234 million (+26% YoY). Thermal and hydroelectric generation represented the majority of its incomes, with $184 million (+36% YoY).

But that quarter wasn't the exception of the year, on the contrary: in the first nine months net income was $226 million, a much higher amount than the $80 million within the same period last year, while revenue was $777 million, 16% more than the same period in 2024.

The bad news of the quarter was the EPS of $0.07, which fell $0.09 short of the consensus estimate of $0.16. However, the EPS grew 43% QoQ and 161% YoY, so this doesn't deny the operating growth. During this year the EPS was $0.15, the highest number of the last 5 years.

Regarding its cash position for operating activities, the company has $158 million and it could drop by the end of the fiscal year, in comparison with 2024, which closed with $285 million. It's worth mentioning that the company is investing $140 million in capital, as I've mentioned before, and I don't consider that this reduction could jeopardize short term finances. In any case Central Puerto has the margin to take on more debt if needed, as I'll show later.

More Opportunities in the Local Energy Market

I don't want to bore you with more positive results, but I wanted to focus on one big piece of news for Central Puerto in Q3, which is a lot of flexibility in the Argentine energy market. The government of Javier Milei is already deregulating the energy market but now, with Resolution 400/25 of the Secretary of Energy, the government will stop monopolizing an important part of the energy market, allowing private companies to buy and sell energy up to 20% in the large users market (the high-consumption industrial segment), and 100% with distributors (regional or provincial companies with mostly regulated prices).

Although we must still understand price restructuring and regulatory laws for new contracts, there are two segments that present new opportunities for market share gains, and potential to obtain better profits through pricing, given that the resolution allows them to raise margins on variable production costs. This is because the market goes (gradually) from regulated prices to a more competitive and free market.

Both in the investor presentation and in the earnings call, Bonell celebrated these announcements and highlighted that incomes are now denominated in US dollars, removing Argentina's exchange rate risk.

Logically, the resolution benefits the entire Argentine energy sector, but Central Puerto could increase its income in a considerable way due to its strong market share in the electricity sector. Demand for electricity is greatest especially in summer in Argentina (December 2025-March 2026), especially for heavy users. According to Bonell the EBITDA increase could reach 25% in the first phase, although it will be much higher if they manage sell 20% to large users. This revenue would be reflected in the short term:

The impact of deregulation could increase EBITDA by 20-25%, depending on dispatch and fuel consumption. The new regulation allows pricing in dollars, reducing dependency on government resolutions for price increases. We are exploring selling 20% to large users, though the market is still adjusting to the new regulations. We expect to reach the 20% target this year and potentially more with distribution companies.

Q: You mentioned a 20-25% increase in EBITDA. Is this without considering the 20% that could be sold to big users?

A: Yes, the 20-25% increase without the 20% sales to big users. If successful in selling that 20%, further improvement is possible.

Manageable Debt and an Opportunity for Argentine's Stabilization

Regarding the capital structure of the company, cash and equivalents were $292 million, leaving net debt of $160 million. Its total gross debt was equivalent to $452 million, so the company has a manageable situation in the medium term, that reflects in a net leverage ratio of 0.5x. At the same time Moody has evaluated the company for the first time and has given it a AA+ rating, while Fix SCR has moved it from AA- to AA.

The situation of the company regarding the debt is clearly healthy, and we could even say that it is fully utilized. The company could even leverage greater capital to invest in new projects. However, the economy of Argentina weighs in, which could put some limits on debt growth aspirations.

The country's risk is usually above 1000 pts and that increases borrowing costs. Anyway, since election day on October 26th the country's risk level has been declining due to the victory of the incumbent government and its pro-market stance and decisions, with country-related risk falling from almost 1100 pts to 600 pts. If this indicator keeps improving, I can see more and more opportunities for the energetic sector in general, and for Central Puerto in particular.

Valuation: Argentine Energy Companies

The growth potential for the company, its investment projects and the new laws of the country, makes Centro Puerto an attractive buying option at current prices ($15-$16). But despite these positive factors, the company is still undervalued, I believe, for two main reasons: the first one is evident, the risks of the Argentine market are very high for the political comes and goes, and the second reason is that energy sector, in general, is not being visible to large, global investors that pay more attention to Big Tech and related AI companies.

But to better calculate some potential scenarios for the company and potential upside, I made a comparative model with other Argentine energy companies that also have very interesting profiles.

If we take a look just at Central Puerto, EV/EBITDA of 7.2x is clearly undervalued against the median sector (12.8x) but we must understand that the sector is comprised of bigger market cap companies. That's why I think we should buy it, first because of its story, and second for its comparison to Argentine peers. In the first case, the undervaluation compared to its average of the last 5 years is 16%, an upside that I think is interesting enough to enter a position on, based on my thesis.

And if we compare it with other Argentine companies, the analysis could lead us to think that the company is a little expensive, especially if we look at P/CF or P/E. But both metrics discount the company's potential due to the recent price jump, so we must pay attention to the EV/EBITDA. If we look closer, Central Puerto is in line with its peers: it's just a little bit more expensive than Vista Energy S.A.B de C.V. (VIST) but cheaper than Pampa Energía S.A. (PAM) and than Transportadora de Gas del Sur S.A. (TGS). The same happens if we look at the P/B. What I mean is although the recent surge and the euphoria for the OpenAI announcement, the company continues to trade at favorable prices.

However, I want to make clear the chosen companies also have some differences. Among them is market cap, where Central Puerto is the smallest of the bunch. Another important difference is that Central Puerto and Pampa are dedicated to the utilities sector, while YPF Sociedad Anónima (YPF), Vista and Transportadora operate in the energy sector. There are also other intra-sector nuances because Transportadora transports gas, while YPF and Vista are more dedicated to oil and gas upstream. Regarding Central Puerto and Pampa, Central Puerto is a producer of electric energy and Pampa is mostly a distributor, although it is also a generator.

These differences change the valuation of the median sector. For example, for the utilities sector, EV/EBITDA is 12.8x (hence Central Puerto's 16% upside, which gives me a target price of $17 approximately). For the energy sector EV/EBITDA is 7.2x. To better understand this comparison, I created another chart with this intra-sector division.

At the same time, all these companies could play a key role in OpenAI's data center investment, but I believe that Central Puerto is better positioned because it is a net generator of electricity, and the demand for an AI data center is expected to be energy-intensive. In this case, Pampa is the biggest competitor because it also has a power plant in Neuquén, for example. However, Central Puerto is the one that has the MoU signed with OpenAI, as I've mentioned at the beginning, and PAMP doesn't.

For its part, YPF and Vista are oil companies, while TGS is a distributor. They could benefit indirectly, but they will play a less significant role in supplying the data center.

Risks

The company's risks are concentrated on the Argentine political instability, which affected the country since September when the incumbent government lost local elections in Buenos Aires. The landscape changed in October when the government won the national elections. But this shows that Argentina, even with Milei and its reforms, is very unstable, and in 2027 we expect the uncertainty will increase with another round of presidential election.

Another political risk is that president Milei could fail if he can't approve the laws that are in the Argentine Congress. While he now has more allies, nothing is guaranteed. Anything could cause tension in the local stocks listed in the US.

And of course there is execution risk related to the OpenAi contract, and any hiccups or delays therein.

Conclusion

If we take a look at the Argentine market at the close of 2025 there are several very attractive options, but Central Puerto is the best in my opinion. And I repeat once again that my investment thesis is not focused just on the OpenAI investment. No. My bet on Central Puerto is because of its potential to supply the growing energy demand of the country. Of course the OpenAI investment is a great catalyst, but my point is that even if it didn't exist, Central Puerto is still a great buying option for the long term.

data.title: AI Is Catalyzing A New Era Of Expansion For Alphabet
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Two weeks ago, Alphabet Inc. ( GOOG) (GOOGL) (GOOG:CA) delivered an impressive quarter, which, in my opinion, is a key reason why I am reiterating a Buy rating for the company. The Search giant's core advertising business is inflecting upward again. And ironically, this is because of AI. In most of my previous articles on some of its peers, such as Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), I said that AI is a key driver for these giant companies. I have also said that AI will be a key catalyst for driving growth in most of these hyperscalers. Specifically, for GOOG, Google Cloud is accelerating as the next growth pillar. And this is a key point that I addressed in my previous Alphabet article. In Q3 2025 Earnings Report, Alphabet's revenues surged 16% y/y to over $102 Bn. And this is the first time it ever hit a 12 figure quarter. The bottom-line is that AI investments are translating into real business results across search, YouTube and cloud business, boosting both the top line and operating leverage, as noted by the CEO, Sundar Pichai:

...We delivered our first ever $100 billion quarter. Five years ago, our quarterly revenue was at $50 billion. Our revenue number has doubled since then, and we are firmly in the generative AI era. In parallel, we've built for the long term and diversified with successful businesses in cloud, YouTube and subscriptions...

My key takeaway from this is that Alphabet's growth engine is revving at scale and AI is catalyzing a new era of expansion. And this is why I am remaining bullish and reiterating my Buy rating. Because GOOG's combination of rejuvenated ad momentum and booming cloud and AI demand makes the company a unique winner in Big Tech. Yes, the stock may not look cheap on traditional metrics, especially given the fact that it scores an F Valuation grade. Nevertheless, its Growth grade shows a different picture. GOOG scores a B growth grade with a ~13% y/y revenue growth, which is far above the sector median of ~3%, as shown below.

Therefore in my view, paying a premium for a business delivering accelerating double digit growth and expanding margins is justified.

Google is scaling Gemini at consumer and enterprise levels fast. Management actually says that Gemini now processes about 7 Bn tokens per minute via direct API calls. And the Gemini app has over 650Mn monthly active users, and user queries are up 3X from Q3. The team also guided the Gemini 3.0 release which was released on Nov 18th. And this keeps the cadence tight. AI Mode in search is also already live globally in over 40 Languages. And this serves over 40Mn active daily users. Also, AI overviews continue to roll out at scale. And I believe this is exactly what I would want to see. Ship speed, usage and an upgrade path baked in. And when I look at a comparative context, Microsoft (MSFT) leans on OpenAI (OPENAI) models inside Copilot. Amazon (AMZN) on the other side is pushing Bedrock and its Anthropic tie up. Meta (META) is also seeding Llama broadly. Google (GOOG) is literally the only one offering both its own frontier models and the first partly silicon at hyperscale. And this matters when enterprises want one stack for training and serving, as noted by the CEO, Sundar Pichai:

...we are firmly in the generative AI era...

And I believe when the CEO said this, my takeaway is that AI is no longer a science project at GOOG anymore. In fact, it is moving revenue, engagement, and roadmap at the same time.

Business Overview

Alphabet's business spans search, advertising, cloud services, YouTube, hardware, and more. And the company's model is a potent blend of massive scale and diversification. Search and other Google services still drive the bulk of revenue, of course (at $87Bn in Q3 2025 which is a 14y/y growth). Then there are other newer engines like Cloud (at $15.2Bn representing a 34% y/y growth). GOOG has also successfully evolved from a one trick search pony into a multi cylinder tech powerhouse. And all this is while leveraging AI in every part of the stack.

GOOG's core ad revenues are back to solid growth. Search ads jumped 15% y/y and YouTube ad sales also climbed 15% as digital ad spending rebounded. From the Earnings call, management highlighted that AI features in search (such as AI overviews and interactive AI mode) are actually driving more user engagement and queries, especially when it comes to younger users. This counters the bear narrative that generative AI would cannibalize GOOG's search traffic. Instead, AI is making search more useful and sticky creating more opportunities to monetize, as noted by the CEO;

...AI is driving an expansionary moment for Search. As people learn what they can do with our new AI experiences, they're increasingly coming back to Search more...

And clearly from this, in my view, AI enhancements are boosting search usage, and this is more of a tailwind for GOOG's ad business rather than a threat.

When I look at YouTube, it remains a juggernaut in Video. It is now #1 in US streaming, according to Nielsen. It even broke a record with NFL live stream where it had over 17Mn concurrent viewers. Short form YouTube Shorts is also monetizing better. And this is aided by new AI-powered creator tools. My point is that Alphabet's legacy platforms, such as search and YouTube, are not standing still. They are also innovating with AI and continuing to grow and print cash.

Of course, GOOG's business overview would not be complete without mentioning Google Cloud and the broader AI ecosystem it plays in. In my previous GOOG article, I majored on Google Cloud and how it is a game changer for Alphabet. Now, imagine combining Google Cloud with the traditional legacy businesses that are also AI-embedded, and the end result is a powerful business in a new era of expansion. The Cloud segment is a key differentiator. Because it had grown from a negligible contributor almost 5 to 6 years ago to a $53Bn annual run rate business. And Google is the only cloud provider offering both top tier AI chips and proprietary models. It is also winning big customers because 9 out of 10 AI research labs chose Google cloud:

...This leadership is winning customers like HCA Healthcare, LG AI Research and Macquarie Bank, and it's why 9 of the top 10 AI labs choose Google Cloud....

...we are signing new customers faster...

...Over 70% of existing Google Cloud customers use our AI products, including Banco BV, Best Buy and FairPrice Group...

Growth drivers

In my view, the biggest growth driver for GOOG now is its central role in the AI and cloud computing ecosystem. And the clever strategies it is using to extend that advantage. GOOG is not just a bystander in the AI boom but rather a primary enabler of AI across hardware, software, and cloud services. Specifically, I see several opportunities in:

First is AI driven Search & Ads. GOOG has turned the AI threat into an opportunity. New AI search experiences (such as overviews and conversational modes) are actually driving usage and queries. And as these AI features roll out globally, then they should continue expanding Search's reach. More queries and engagements ultimately mean more ad impressions to monetize. And the bottom line is that AI is actually expanding GOOG's core and TAM by unlocking new commercial intent rather than shrinking it.

Secondly is the ecosystem positioning. I say this because GOOG sits at an advantageous nexus in the AI ecosystem. For example, Google is a top client of Nvidia (NVDA) [for GPUs] and a competitor via TPUs. However, this dual approach implies that it wins either way as AI compute demand increases. It also partners with OpenAI (OPENAI) indirectly via NVDA's investments there while also competing via Google DeepMind and Gemini. It is also cooperating with Meta (META) on cloud infrastructure even as Meta develops its own AI models (Llama) that rivals Googles. Also, let me not forget about Amazon (AMZN) which just announced its own $4Bn investment in Anthropic to ensure AWS. This means that AI arms race is quite intense. But Alphabet's strategy of open handed partnerships could make it a central backbone for many AI Ventures.

Alphabet is also investing ahead of demand. Q3 capex was $24Bn and the full year 2025 capex is now guided at between $91Bn to $93Bn. This is about 60% in servers and 40% in data centers and networking. Depreciation rose $1.6Bn y/y to $5.6Bn and I believe this will set up again as new capacity comes online. Management also flagged a tight supply-demand ecosystem, especially moving into 2026. And this is exactly why capex is front loaded. And I believe cash flow can handle it. Because the trailing 12th-month cash flow sits around $73.6Bn even after the step in spending.

When I look at the pipeline, GOOG's cloud reached $155Bn which was up 46% sequentially and 82% y/y. And the team has signed more than $1Bn deals through Q3 than in the last 2 previous years combined, and 13 product lines now run at $1Bn ARR each. New GCP customers rose 34% y/y and 70% of the existing customers already use GOOG's AI products. And the revenue from products built on GOOG's gen AI models grew 200% y/y. This I believe is a healthy order book by any company's yardstick.

It is also important to mention that GOOG is scaling both partner GPU and its own TPUs. The new A4X Max instances powered by Nvidia (NVDA) GB300 are shipping to cloud customers, and GOOG's 7th gen TPU (commonly known as Ironwood) is also coming to general availability. GOOG is also reserving capacity for ecosystem wins. Anthropic plans to access up to 1 Mn TPUs. And I believe this is a visible example of GOOG's full-stack positioning.

Lastly, there is Google Cloud, which I will not focus much on since I covered it in my previous article on GOOG.

Valuation

Like I said before, GOOG's valuation numbers may not look decent as of now; however, I still find them reasonable relative to peers and its own growth. For instance, when I look at the FWD PEG ratio, GOOG stands at 1.85x, which ideally is priced at 1.8 times its Long term earnings growth rate. And this is very similar to Amazon (AMZN) at 1.81x and below Microsoft's (MSFT) 2.36x. Meta's (META) PEG is 1.52x given its faster growth off a lower base.

My point is that Alphabet's PEG of 1.8x suggests that the stock is not pricing in outrageous growth. It is roughly in line with Amazon (AMZN) and at a ~27% discount to Microsoft's (MSFT) PEG.

Again, when I look at FWD Non-GAAP P/E, I note that GOOG stands at ~27x (almost similar to META's) versus 31x for MSFT and 34x for AMZN. Alphabet is about 10% to 20% cheaper than those peers on FWD earnings. And this shows that Alphabet is trading at a discount relative to its peers. Comparing to the sector median, GOOG is trading at a premium since the median is 15x. So this means that GOOG is trading at a 75% premium to the average Comm Service company. Yet, the sector includes slow growth companies. Nevertheless, for a mega cap like GOOG, which is growing at 30% [EPS] I would say that a 28X FWD Non-GAAP P/E is not excessive in my view.

Alphabet's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for GOOG as of now is that the Gen AI frenzy is a double-edged sword. This means that if alternative AI search or chatbot tools were to start displacing GOOG for certain queries, then it would certainly pressure search usage. However, so far, GOOG's AI enhancement seems to be expanding its moat and not eroding it. However, it is important to note that the landscape can change fast in tech.

Final Thoughts

Alphabet has reasserted itself as a top-tier growth story in Big Tech. And this has been powered by the virtuous cycle of AI driving more usage, which eventually drives more revenue. And funds more AI innovation. And Q3 2025 results underscored that both arms of the business, that is ads and cloud, are thriving. And this is why I am reiterating my Buy rating for Alphabet Inc.
